papers.title: Model Agreement via Anchoring
papers.abstract: Numerous lines of aim to control $\textit{model disagreement}$ -- the extent to which two machine learning models disagree in their predictions. We adopt a simple and standard notion of model disagreement in real-valued prediction problems, namely the expected squared difference in predictions between two models trained on independent samples, without any coordination of the training processes. We would like to be able to drive disagreement to zero with some natural parameter(s) of the training procedure using analyses that can be applied to existing training methodologies.   We develop a simple general technique for proving bounds on independent model disagreement based on $\textit{anchoring}$ to the average of two models within the analysis. We then apply this technique to prove disagreement bounds for four commonly used machine learning algorithms: (1) stacked aggregation over an arbitrary model class (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the number of models $k$ being stacked) (2) gradient boosting (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the number of iterations $k$) (3) neural network training with architecture search (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the size $n$ of the architecture being optimized over) and (4) regression tree training over all regression trees of fixed depth (where disagreement is driven to 0 with the depth $d$ of the tree architecture). For clarity, we work out our initial bounds in the setting of one-dimensional regression with squared error loss -- but then show that all of our results generalize to multi-dimensional regression with any strongly convex loss.

2602.23360v1.full_summary:
The research paper "Model Agreement via Anchoring" addresses the challenge of model disagreement, where two machine learning models trained on independent samples from the same distribution produce different predictions. This phenomenon, often referred to as predictive multiplicity, the Rashomon effect, or model churn, poses significant problems for the reliability, fairness, and replicability of machine learning systems. When different models offer different predictions for the same input, it creates ambiguity in downstream decision-making and can lead to arbitrary outcomes in high-stakes settings. The authors seek to identify natural training parameters that can drive this disagreement to zero using analyses that apply to existing, practical algorithms rather than specialized, impractical ones.

The core methodology introduced is a general technique called "midpoint anchoring." This approach is built upon a mathematical identity for squared loss which demonstrates that the expected squared difference between two models is proportional to the gap between the average of their individual losses and the loss of their midpoint average. While many machine learning model classes are non-convex—meaning the average of two models in a class might not reside within that same class—the authors observe that the average can often be represented by a slightly more complex version of the same architecture. By "anchoring" the analysis to this hypothetical midpoint model, the authors relate model disagreement to the "local learning curve." This curve measures how much a model’s performance improves when its complexity (such as size or depth) is increased.

The authors apply this anchoring technique to four widely used machine learning frameworks. First, in stacked aggregation (stacking), they show that disagreement between two independent ensembles can be driven to zero as the number of models being stacked increases. The stability of the ensemble is tied to the marginal improvement gained by doubling the number of base models. Second, they analyze gradient boosting, proving that disagreement diminishes at a rate of $O(1/k)$, where $k$ is the number of iterations. Unlike stacking, gradient boosting is path-dependent and adaptive, yet the anchoring method still successfully bounds the disagreement between independent runs.

The third and fourth applications cover highly non-convex settings: neural networks with architecture search and regression trees. For ReLU neural networks, the average of two networks with $n$ nodes can be represented by a network with $2n$ nodes. Similarly, the average of two regression trees of depth $d$ can be represented by a tree of depth $2d$. In both cases, the authors prove that if the local learning curve flattens—meaning the error does not drop significantly when doubling the size or depth—the models must exhibit high levels of predictive agreement. These results are "agnostic," meaning they hold regardless of whether the models achieve high absolute accuracy or whether the underlying data distribution is simple or complex.

Beyond these specific applications, the paper provides a proof of the "tightness" of their results. They demonstrate that for stacked aggregation, the constant factor in their upper bound cannot be generically improved, confirming the robustness of the anchoring technique. Furthermore, while the initial analysis focuses on one-dimensional regression with squared error loss, the authors successfully generalize all their findings to multi-dimensional regression problems utilizing any strongly convex loss function.

Despite the strength of these theoretical guarantees, certain limitations remain evident. While the bounds on disagreement are independent of the specific instance or problem complexity, the rate at which a learning curve flattens is still problem-dependent. In some scenarios, a learning curve might remain steep for a long period before flattening, meaning high levels of complexity might be required to guarantee agreement. Additionally, the results for neural networks and regression trees assume the ability to find approximately optimal models within those classes, which can be computationally intensive in practice. Finally, the analysis specifically targets agreement in "prediction space" rather than "parameter space," meaning that while two models might agree on their outputs, their internal structures and weights may still remain fundamentally different.
2602.23360v1.brief: Midpoint anchoring minimizes predictive disagreement by linking performance gains to increased model complexity.
2602.23360v1.summary:
* The paper examines why independent models often produce conflicting predictions for the same data.
* It introduces "midpoint anchoring" to link model disagreement to local learning curves.
* Predictive agreement increases when doubling model complexity yields diminishing performance gains.
* The authors provide stability guarantees for neural networks, gradient boosting, and regression trees.
* This research establishes theoretical bounds for model reliability across standard learning frameworks.

comments.text: The authors’ use of midpoint anchoring to link model stability to the local learning curve is a genuinely clever bit of theoretical footwork. It provides a remarkably elegant, unified lens for analyzing predictive multiplicity across such disparate architectures.

It's such a clever move to link predictive agreement to the local learning curve, though I’m low-key stressed about the "doubling complexity" part. I've spent way too many nights staring at two models with the same loss that still disagree on edge cases, so it’s nice to see some theory actually tackling that chaos.

I’d use this to stop those awkward "why did the score change?" meetings with stakeholders after a simple model refresh. I want to see if doubling my ensemble size finally kills the prediction jitter that drove me crazy on my last credit scoring project.

I’m a bit skeptical because doubling complexity to force agreement feels like a brute-force bandage that doesn't actually fix underlying data biases. I’ve seen plenty of massive models agree on the exact same hallucination, so just getting them to match their outputs doesn't mean we're any closer to the truth.

While we’ve long treated predictive multiplicity as a spooky, irreducible ghost in the machine, this work finally grounds model agreement in the gritty reality of learning curve saturation. It’s a sharp, refreshing pivot that shows how standard, high-capacity architectures naturally stop bickering once you hit those inevitable diminishing returns in complexity.
